evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Specific human voice vs committee 3/10 — Reads as corporate marketing copy with interchangeable editorial byline.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 4/10 — Has one or two opinionated lines but mostly hedges behind guidelines.
Unexpected phrasing 2/10 — Relies on tired wellness clichés like 'progress not perfection' and 'close the loop'.
Specific citations 5/10 — Mentions DHA, MOHAP, BMJ, Lancet and one price, but citations are unlinked placeholders and details are thin.
Point of view 3/10 — Surveys guidelines evenly without taking a stance on what actually matters.
Quotable sentence 2/10 — Nothing memorable; language is bureaucratic and brand-laden.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with a TL;DR block and generic framing rather than a hook.

specificity 6/10 — The post has good pockets of specificity (AED prices, 50/25/25 plate split, khubz, taraweeh) but repeatedly lapses into vague claims — 'measurably more effective,' 'changeable calendars,' 'better adaptation,' 'improves adherence' — without citing the actual statistic or naming the study finding. The flagged sentence is the worst offender: 'measurably more effective' promises a number but delivers nothing. Several other sentences in the nutrition and behaviour-change sections share this flaw (e.g., 'significantly improve long-term adherence,' 'more pronounced among adults managing wellness in changeable calendars'). Each claim should be anchored to a named study finding, a percentage, or a concrete comparator.
